name: Miami-Dade County Public Schools
districtFact: Fourth largest school district in the United States, serving approximately 330,000 students across more than 550 schools in Miami-Dade County.

about.title: About Miami-Dade County Public Schools
about.content: Miami-Dade County Public Schools (M-DCPS) is the fourth largest school district in the United States, serving approximately 330,000 students across more than 550 schools. The district covers Miami-Dade County in its entirety — from Homestead and Florida City at the southern edge through the core Miami urban area, north through Hialeah and Miami Lakes, and east across the barrier islands to Miami Beach and Key Biscayne. School assignment follows attendance zones that track county geography, not city names or mailing addresses. Families in Kendall, Hialeah, and Coral Gables all attend M-DCPS, but their assigned schools, transportation options, and feeder patterns can be very different.

about.title: What to Know Before the First School Year
about.content: Two features of the M-DCPS calendar aren't obvious from the outside. The official calendar is published in English, Spanish, and Haitian Creole — all three versions at dadeschools.net carry identical dates and are equally authoritative. Teacher planning days are student holidays but don't appear on standard US holiday lists; they're labeled separately in the official calendar. Relying on informal school communications or generic holiday schedules to track no-school days will miss them. The school year runs on two semesters; the first semester closes in December before Winter Break begins, not after the holiday ends.

content: Teacher planning days in M-DCPS are student holidays but don't always fall on named federal or state holidays. The official calendar at dadeschools.net labels them separately from class days — check it directly when a no-school day doesn't correspond to a named holiday.

M-DCPS graduation ceremonies for high school seniors take place in late May, typically several weeks before the last instructional day for other students. Parents of seniors should check the official calendar for the graduation timeline, which is published separately from the regular school year end date.

compareIntro: Compare the Miami-Dade school calendar with nearby and high-demand Florida district calendars currently available on MySchoolDates. Families most often compare Miami-Dade with Broward and Palm Beach because the three South Florida counties set independent calendars, and Spring Break, teacher planning days, hurricane makeup days, and last-day timing do not always match. Orange County is included as a Central Florida reference for families planning theme-park travel around school breaks.

districtFaqs.q: Does Miami-Dade have makeup days for hurricanes?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. Florida law allows school districts to bank up to three emergency makeup days into the calendar. M-DCPS builds these days into its schedule to absorb short-term storm closures without extending the year. If a major hurricane causes school closures beyond the banked allotment, the district may add makeup days at the end of the year, potentially pushing the last day past the published June date. M-DCPS announces schedule changes through dadeschools.net, district notifications, and local television.

districtFaqs.q: Why does Miami-Dade end the school year later than most Florida districts?
districtFaqs.a: M-DCPS ends the school year in early June — the 2026-2027 last day is June 3 — reflecting the district's instructional hour requirements, teacher planning day schedule, and the hurricane buffer built into the calendar structure. Broward and Palm Beach counties typically end in late May. The later end date can shift further if hurricane makeup days are added during the year. Plan summer activities around a June end rather than the late-May finish common in other Florida and US districts.

districtFaqs.q: Is Miami-Dade Spring Break the same week as Broward County?
districtFaqs.a: Not always. In 2026-2027, Miami-Dade, Broward, and Palm Beach all share Spring Break the week of March 22-26, but this alignment varies by year. In years when the three South Florida counties are on different weeks, breaks run on consecutive weeks — which directly affects households with children in more than one county's schools, or parents whose work schedules are tied to one district's calendar. Check each county's official calendar for the specific year rather than assuming they match.

districtFaqs.q: What are teacher planning days in Miami-Dade?
districtFaqs.a: Teacher planning days in M-DCPS are days when staff report to school but students do not. They appear on the district calendar as student holidays, but are labeled separately from state and federal holidays. The number and placement varies by school year. The M-DCPS calendar at dadeschools.net is the reliable source for all student no-school days, including planning days that don't fall on named holidays.

districtFaqs.q: How do I find my child's assigned school in Miami-Dade?
districtFaqs.a: School assignment in M-DCPS is based on your home address and the current attendance zone for your campus level. The district's School Locator tool at dadeschools.net identifies your assigned elementary, middle, and high school by address. Miami-Dade's school zones follow county geography rather than city names — a Hialeah mailing address and a Miami mailing address can both fall in the same attendance zone, or in different schools depending on the specific street. Neighborhood descriptions and real estate materials often lag behind current zone boundaries; the School Locator is the current source.

districtFaqs.q: Does M-DCPS publish the school calendar in Spanish?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. The official M-DCPS school calendar is published in English, Spanish, and Haitian Creole — all three versions are available for download at dadeschools.net and carry the same dates. For households where English is not the primary language, the Spanish or Haitian Creole version is typically the most usable day-to-day reference.

livingHere.intro: Miami-Dade County's school calendar carries several features that don't apply in most other US districts: a school year that runs into June, a Spring Break that may or may not align with neighboring Broward and Palm Beach counties, a hurricane season that overlaps with the first two months of school, and an official calendar published in three languages.

highlights.label: Hurricane Season
highlights.detail:
Miami-Dade County sits in the most hurricane-exposed corridor in the continental United States. Peak Atlantic hurricane season runs August through October — concurrent with the first two months of the school year. Florida law requires districts to bank up to three emergency makeup days into the calendar; M-DCPS does this, meaning short-duration storm closures typically don't extend the school year. A major storm that forces closures beyond the banked days can add makeup days at the end of the year, pushing the last day past the published June date.

Families moving from areas with little hurricane experience are often surprised that most school closures last only a day or two — storm preparation and passage, then school resumes. Extended multi-week closures are rare but not unknown. M-DCPS communicates schedule changes through dadeschools.net, district email and text alerts, and local television. Signing up for district notifications in August, before the peak storm window opens, is how most Miami-Dade parents stay ahead of sudden schedule changes.

highlights.label: Spring Break Alignment
highlights.detail: When Miami-Dade, Broward, and Palm Beach all observe the same Spring Break week — as in 2026-2027 — South Florida parents with children across multiple county districts are on the same schedule. In years when the counties stagger their breaks, the weeks run consecutively. The alignment changes year to year. Parents coordinating childcare or plans across county lines are better off checking each district's published calendar than assuming the counties match.

highlights.label: School Year End in June
highlights.detail: M-DCPS ends the school year in early June — later than Broward, Palm Beach, and most US school districts. The published last day for 2026-2027 is June 3, but hurricane makeup days can shift that further. The June end compresses the gap between the last day of school and many summer program start dates. It's a date that catches first-year Miami-Dade families off guard; most local summer programs account for it, but programs and camps based outside South Florida often don't.

highlights.label: Multilingual Calendar
highlights.detail: The official M-DCPS school calendar is published in English, Spanish, and Haitian Creole, with all three versions available at dadeschools.net. Many families prefer the Spanish or Haitian Creole version for everyday reference. All three carry identical dates — teacher planning days, holidays, early release days, and break periods are consistent across language versions.

relatedDistricts.name: Broward County Public Schools
relatedDistricts.reason: Miami-Dade and Broward Spring Break dates don't always align — in years when they're staggered, the two counties' breaks run on consecutive weeks, directly affecting parents with children in both districts or whose schedules are tied to one county's calendar.

relatedDistricts.name: Palm Beach County School District
relatedDistricts.reason: Palm Beach County's Spring Break and school year end timing tracks closely with Miami-Dade's but can differ; all three South Florida counties sharing the same break week is not guaranteed annually.

relatedDistricts.name: Orange County Public Schools
relatedDistricts.reason: Orange County is a Central Florida reference point for Miami-Dade families planning Orlando and theme-park travel during Winter Break, Spring Break, or long student holidays.
